title: Jira Custom Fields Architecture and Scoping
topic: Jira custom fields architecture — how Jira defines, scopes, and stores custom fields across projects and issue types, including field contexts, field configurations, field configuration schemes, and how values are stored per issue
summary: How Jira layers field definition, context scoping, options, behaviour config, and EAV value storage — and what Vector should adapt.
content:
Contents
  1. Overview
  2. Key Findings
  3. Detailed Analysis
  4. Architecture Summary
  5. Implications for Vector Custom Field Design
  6. Gaps
  7. Sources

Overview

Jira uses a layered architecture to manage custom fields: a single field definition is shared globally, then scoped to specific projects and issue types via contexts, configured for required/optional/hidden behaviour via field configurations, and grouped into schemes that attach to projects. Values are stored in a flat EAV (entity-attribute-value) table per issue. This separation allows one field to behave differently across teams without duplicating the field definition.

Key Findings

  • Custom fields are defined once globally, then scoped via contexts — not duplicated per project
  • A context = a (projects x issue types) intersection — controls which options and defaults appear
  • Only one context can apply to a given project for a given field — no overlapping scope
  • Field configurations control behaviour (required, optional, hidden) per field per issue type
  • Field configuration schemes group configurations and attach to projects
  • All values stored in a single customfieldvalue EAV table with typed columns (STRING, NUMBER, TEXT, DATE)
  • Multi-value fields (multi-select, multi-user) produce multiple rows per issue per field
  • Options for select-type fields are context-scoped — different option sets per context

Detailed Analysis

Layer 1 — Field Definition (customfield table)

A custom field is a single row in customfield. It has a type key (e.g. com.atlassian.jira.plugin.system.customfieldtypes:textfield), a name, and an optional description. The field exists once for the entire Jira instance. All scoping, options, and behaviour are applied downstream through contexts and configurations — never by duplicating the field row.

ColumnPurpose
IDPK
cfkeyStable string key (e.g. customfield_10020)
CUSTOMFIELDTYPEKEYPlugin type — drives rendering and storage
cfnameDisplay label
DESCRIPTIONAdmin-facing description

Layer 2 — Context (fieldconfigscheme + configurationcontext + fieldconfigschemeissuetype)

A context = a field configuration scheme scoped to a set of projects and issue types. It controls two things: (1) which options appear in select-type fields, and (2) what the default value is. A global context applies to all projects; a project-specific context applies only to listed projects and overrides the global context for those projects.

TablePurpose
fieldconfigschemeNamed context — linked to a custom field
configurationcontextMaps a scheme to a project (NULL PROJECT = global)
fieldconfigschemeissuetypeMaps a scheme to an issue type (NULL = all types)

Constraint: Only one context can apply to a single project for a single field. Once a project is in a context, it cannot be added to another context for the same field. Project-specific contexts always override the global context.

Layer 3 — Options (customfieldoption table)

For select-type fields, options are stored in customfieldoption, linked to a specific CUSTOMFIELDCONFIG (the field configuration scheme). This means Workspace A and Workspace B can have completely different option lists for the same select field — the options are context-scoped, not global to the field.

ColumnPurpose
CUSTOMFIELDFK to field definition
CUSTOMFIELDCONFIGFK to field config scheme — scopes the option to a context
PARENTOPTIONIDFor cascading selects — parent option
SEQUENCEDisplay order
customvalueThe option label/value
disabledSoft-disable without deleting

Layer 4 — Field Configuration + Scheme (behaviour)

Separate from contexts, field configurations control whether a field is required, optional, or hidden for a given issue type. A field configuration scheme groups multiple field configurations and is attached to a project. This is orthogonal to contexts — context controls options/defaults; field configuration controls required/hidden behaviour.

Layer 5 — Values (customfieldvalue table — EAV)

All custom field values are stored in a single flat table. One row per (issue x field), except for multi-value fields which produce multiple rows. The type key on the field definition determines which typed column is populated.

ColumnPurpose
ISSUEFK to issue
CUSTOMFIELDFK to field definition
STRINGVALUEText, URL, single-select values
NUMBERVALUENumeric fields, story points
TEXTVALUELong text / rich text
DATEVALUEDate / datetime fields
VALUETYPEDiscriminator for multi-value rows
PARENTKEYFor cascading values — links child to parent option

Multi-select and multi-user-picker fields produce one customfieldvalue row per selected value, all sharing the same ISSUE + CUSTOMFIELD combination.

Default Values

Default values are stored in genericconfiguration with DATATYPE = DefaultValue and an XMLVALUE column containing the serialised default. Defaults are context-scoped — different contexts can have different defaults for the same field.

Architecture Summary

customfield (field definition — one row per field, global)
    |
    +-- fieldconfigscheme (context — scopes field to projects x issue types)
    |       +-- configurationcontext (project scope — NULL = all projects)
    |       +-- fieldconfigschemeissuetype (issue type scope — NULL = all types)
    |
    +-- customfieldoption (options — per context, not global)
    |
    +-- customfieldvalue (values — per issue, EAV, typed columns)
            ISSUE x CUSTOMFIELD -> STRINGVALUE | NUMBERVALUE | TEXTVALUE | DATEVALUE

Implications for Vector Custom Field Design

Jira's pattern is instructive for Vector's artefact schema. The key lessons:

  • Define fields once, scope per workspace — Jira never duplicates the field definition; scoping is a separate concern. Vector's schema table should be the single definition layer, with subscription_id as the scope key.
  • Options are scope-scoped, not field-scoped — A select field's options differ per workspace. Vector needs an options sub-table on the schema row, not a global options list.
  • Values are a flat EAV table with typed columns — Jira's customfieldvalue uses STRINGVALUE, NUMBERVALUE, TEXTVALUE, DATEVALUE rather than a single polymorphic TEXT column. This avoids casting and enables native Postgres indexes per type. Vector's value table should follow the same pattern.
  • Behaviour (required/hidden) is orthogonal to options/defaults — Vector should keep required, position, and default_value on the schema row, and options in a sub-table.
  • One context per project constraint — Jira enforces this at the DB layer. Vector enforces it via UNIQUE(subscription_id, field_name) on the schema table — same effect.

Gaps

  • Jira Cloud internal DB schema not publicly documented — the customfieldvalue structure above is from Jira Data Center/Server docs; Cloud may differ internally
  • How Jira handles field schema migrations (renaming a field, changing its type) is not covered in public docs
  • Performance characteristics of the customfieldvalue EAV table at scale not documented (known community concern for large instances)

Sources

  1. Atlassian Developer Docs — Database: Custom Fields
  2. Jira Data Center 11.3 — Configuring custom field contexts
  3. Jira Cloud REST API — Issue Custom Field Contexts
  4. Atlassian Support — Field configuration schemes
  5. Atlassian Support — Retrieve custom field configs and values from DB
